title: Missing coverage file silently passes the ratchet check
rationale: When readCoverage returns null (file not found or Unknown metrics), the check() function silently continues without failing. If CI fails to produce coverage-summary.json for a package (e.g., test:coverage misconfiguration or a build error swallowed by turbo), the ratchet will pass with zero verification for that package. This defeats the purpose of the ratchet in failure scenarios. Consider failing (or at least warning loudly) when a baseline exists for a package but no coverage data was produced.
suggestion: In check(), when actual is null but baseline exists, increment failures or emit a prominent warning. Example: if (!actual && baseline) { console.error(`  FAIL: ${pkgKey} has baseline but no coverage data was produced`); failures++; }
evidence: readCoverage returns null on missing file (line 46-48) or Unknown metrics (line 39-41)
check() skips null results with continue (line 73-75), even when baselines exist for the package

title: NaN baseline values would silently pass the comparison
rationale: If a baseline value were somehow NaN (e.g., manual edit of coverage-baselines.json), the comparison actualVal < baselineVal evaluates to false for NaN, so the ratchet would pass. The readCoverage function guards against non-number pct values, but loadBaselines performs no validation on the JSON it reads. This is low risk since the baselines file is machine-generated, but a schema check on loadBaselines would make it robust.
suggestion: Add a Number.isFinite check: if (Number.isFinite(baselineVal) && actualVal < baselineVal)
evidence: loadBaselines() does JSON.parse with no schema validation (line 53)

title: agents/skills package has test:coverage but is absent from PACKAGES map
rationale: agents/skills/package.json defines a test:coverage script, so turbo run test:coverage will produce coverage output there. However coverage-ratchet.mjs does not include agents/skills in its PACKAGES map, meaning its coverage will never be ratcheted. This may be intentional (perhaps agents/skills is experimental), but it creates a blind spot.
suggestion: Either add 'agents/skills' to the PACKAGES map, or add a comment explaining the exclusion.
evidence: PACKAGES map lists only packages/* entries, not agents/*

title: GitHub Actions ternary expression syntax is correct but fragile
rationale: The expression ${{ matrix.os == 'ubuntu-latest' && 'pnpm test:ci' || 'pnpm test' }} relies on the JavaScript-style short-circuit ternary pattern. This works because 'pnpm test:ci' is truthy. If someone later changed the truthy branch to an empty string or 0, the fallback would incorrectly trigger. This pattern is idiomatic in GitHub Actions, so marking as a note only.
suggestion: No change needed. The pattern is standard for GitHub Actions and works correctly here.
evidence: GitHub Actions expression evaluation: non-empty strings are truthy
This is the documented pattern for conditional values in GHA

title: Doctor refactor is clean -- extract-and-map reduces nesting as intended
rationale: The refactor from a for-loop with nested try/catch to countCommandFiles + platforms.map is a clear improvement. The extracted function handles the error case (returning 0), and the map produces the same results. The as const annotations are correct for the literal types. No issues found.
evidence: map replaces imperative for-loop with push (line 62-77)
Test refactoring mirrors production code simplification
